questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The perceived size of an object by an eye is primarily determined by
options: the distance of the object from the eye
the size of the image produced on the retina
the true size of the object
questions.solution:
The perceived size of an object depends on the visual angle subtended by the object at the unaided eye.

The visual angle directly determines the size of the image formed on the retina. A larger visual angle results in a larger image on the retina, making the object appear larger.

Therefore, the perceived size of an object by an eye is primarily determined by the size of the image produced on the retina.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The muscles of a normal eye experience the least strain when focusing on an object that is
options: at about $1 \text{ m}$ from the eye
at about $25 \text{ cm}$ from the eye
questions.solution:
When a normal eye focuses on an object placed at infinity (far away), the ciliary muscles are completely relaxed. In this state, the focal length of the eye lens is at its maximum, and the eye is in an unaccommodated state.

Conversely, when focusing on nearby objects, the ciliary muscles contract to increase the curvature of the lens, which causes strain. The maximum strain occurs when the object is at the near point (about $25 \text{ cm}$).

Thus, the muscles experience the least strain when the object is far away from the eye.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a normal eye, objects closer than $25 \text{ cm}$ cannot be seen clearly because
options: the eye is incapable of decreasing its focal length beyond a certain limit.
the eye's focal length is $25 \text{ cm}$.
the eye is incapable of decreasing the distance between the eye-lens and the retina beyond a certain limit.
the distance between the eye-lens and the retina is $25 \text{ cm}$.
questions.solution:
The ciliary muscles control the curvature of the eye lens to adjust its focal length. When an object is brought closer to the eye, the ciliary muscles contract to make the lens thicker, which decreases its focal length and allows a sharp image to form on the retina.

However, the ciliary muscles can only contract up to a certain maximum limit. Consequently, the focal length of the eye lens cannot be decreased below a certain minimum value. For a normal eye, this minimum focal length corresponds to an object distance of $25 \text{ cm}$, known as the least distance of distinct vision.

Therefore, objects closer than $25 \text{ cm}$ cannot be seen clearly because the eye is incapable of decreasing its focal length beyond this limit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person Vikram can clearly see objects situated between $25 \text{ cm}$ and $200 \text{ cm}$. Which of the following may represent the range of clear vision for Rohan, who has stronger muscles than Vikram, but all other parameters of his eye are identical to those of Vikram?
questions.solution:
The near point of an eye is determined by the maximum accommodation power of the eye lens, which is achieved when the ciliary muscles are fully contracted.

Stronger ciliary muscles can contract more, increasing the maximum curvature and power of the eye lens. This allows the eye to focus on closer objects, decreasing the near point distance. Thus, Rohan's near point will be less than $25 \text{ cm}$.

The far point is determined by the minimum power of the eye lens, which occurs when the ciliary muscles are fully relaxed.

Since all other parameters of the eye are identical, the fully relaxed state of the lens is the same for both individuals. The strength of the muscles does not affect the relaxed state of the lens.

Therefore, the far point remains unchanged at $200 \text{ cm}$.

The range of clear vision for Rohan could be $18 \text{ cm}$ to $200 \text{ cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the eye observes objects located at various distances, which of the following remains constant?
options: The radii of curvature of the eye-lens.
The focal length of the eye-lens.
The distance of the object from the eye-lens.
The distance of the image from the eye-lens.
questions.solution:
When the eye observes objects at various distances, the object distance $u$ changes.

To form a sharp image on the retina, the eye-lens adjusts its focal length $f$ by changing its radii of curvature with the help of ciliary muscles. This process is known as accommodation.

Since the position of the retina relative to the eye-lens is fixed, the distance of the image from the eye-lens always remains constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The focal length of a normal eye-lens is approximately
questions.solution:
The distance between the eye lens and the retina in a normal human eye is approximately $2 \text{ cm}$.

For viewing distant objects, the object distance is $u = -\infty$.

Using the lens formula, $\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

Substituting $v = 2 \text{ cm}$ and $u = -\infty$, we get $\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{-\infty} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

$f = 2 \text{ cm}$

Thus, the focal length of a normal eye-lens is approximately $2 \text{ cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance from the eye-lens to the retina is $x$. For a normal eye, the maximum focal length of the eye-lens is
questions.solution:
For a normal eye, the maximum focal length of the eye-lens corresponds to the fully relaxed state of the ciliary muscles, which occurs when viewing an object at infinity.

Using the lens formula:

$\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

Here, the object distance $u = -\infty$ and the image is formed on the retina, so the image distance $v = x$.

Substituting these values into the lens formula:

$\dfrac{1}{x} - \dfrac{1}{-\infty} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{max}}}$

$\dfrac{1}{x} - 0 = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{max}}}$

$f_{\text{max}} = x$

Thus, the maximum focal length of the eye-lens is equal to $x$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person using spectacles with a focal length of $+1 \text{ m}$ is unable to see objects clearly beyond $1 \text{ m}$. This situation occurs
options: if the person's vision is normal
questions.solution:
Using the lens formula:

$\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{f}$

Given the focal length of the spectacles $f = +1 \text{ m}$ and the maximum object distance $u = -1 \text{ m}$:

$\dfrac{1}{v} - \dfrac{1}{-1} = \dfrac{1}{1}$

$\dfrac{1}{v} + 1 = 1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{v} = 0 \Rightarrow v = \infty$

The spectacles form the image of the object at $1 \text{ m}$ at infinity. Since the person can see this image clearly but nothing beyond it, their far point must be exactly at infinity.

A far point at infinity is the characteristic of an eye with normal vision.

For a nearsighted (myopic) eye, the far point is at a finite distance, so the maximum visible distance with these spectacles would be less than $1 \text{ m}$.

For a farsighted (hypermetropic) eye, the far point is virtual, allowing the person to see objects beyond $1 \text{ m}$ with these spectacles.

Therefore, this situation occurs if the person's vision is normal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When an object is positioned at a distance $u$ from a simple microscope having a focal length $f$, the resulting angular magnification depends
questions.solution:
The angular magnification $m$ of a simple microscope is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended by the image at the eye ($\theta_i$) to the angle subtended by the object at the unaided eye when placed at the least distance of distinct vision $D$ ($\theta_o$).

Assuming the eye is close to the lens, the angle subtended by the image is $\theta_i = \dfrac{h'}{v}$. Using the linear magnification formula $h' = h \left(\dfrac{v}{u}\right)$, we get $\theta_i = \dfrac{h(v/u)}{v} = \dfrac{h}{u}$.

The angle subtended by the object at the unaided eye is $\theta_o = \dfrac{h}{D}$.

Therefore, the angular magnification is $m = \dfrac{\theta_i}{\theta_o} = \dfrac{h/u}{h/D} = \dfrac{D}{u}$.

Thus, for a given object distance $u$, the angular magnification depends only on $u$ (and the constant $D$), and is independent of the focal length $f$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man views a small object located at his near point. Without altering the positions of his eye or the object, he places a simple microscope with a magnifying power of $5 \text{ X}$ in front of his eyes. The angular magnification achieved is
questions.solution:
The angular magnification (or magnifying power) is defined as the ratio of the angle subtended by the image at the eye ($\theta_i$) to the angle subtended by the object at the unaided eye when placed at the near point ($\theta_o$).

When the object is viewed with the unaided eye at the near point $D$, the angle subtended is:

$\theta_o = \dfrac{h}{D}$

When the simple microscope (convex lens) is placed in front of the eye without altering the object's position, the object distance from the lens remains $u = D$.

The angle subtended by the image at the eye (assuming the lens is very close to the eye) is:

$\theta_i = \dfrac{h'}{v}$

Using the transverse magnification formula for a lens, $\dfrac{h'}{h} = \dfrac{v}{u}$, we can rewrite this as:

$\dfrac{h'}{v} = \dfrac{h}{u}$

Since the object is still at the near point, $u = D$. Therefore, the angle subtended by the image is:

$\theta_i = \dfrac{h}{D}$

The angular magnification achieved in this specific position is:

$M = \dfrac{\theta_i}{\theta_o} = \dfrac{h/D}{h/D} = 1$

The given magnifying power of $5 \text{ X}$ is the maximum magnifying power (when the object is moved closer to the lens such that the image forms at the near point), which does not apply here since the object's position was not altered.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In order to increase the angular magnification of a simple microscope, one must increase
options: the focal length of the lens
questions.solution:
The angular magnification of a simple microscope is given by $M = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f}$ or $M = \dfrac{D}{f}$, where $D$ is the least distance of distinct vision and $f$ is the focal length of the lens.

To increase the angular magnification, the focal length $f$ must be decreased.

Since the power of a lens is given by $P = \dfrac{1}{f}$, decreasing the focal length is equivalent to increasing the power of the lens.

Therefore, to increase the angular magnification, one must increase the power of the lens.
